instruction_en: How do I plan for the farming season before it begins?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mesiesie bere a ansa na ɛba?
response_en: Season planning should include: checking your budget and available resources, deciding which crops to grow based on market demand and land suitability, calculating input quantities needed and ordering in advance, arranging labor supply for peak periods, planning irrigation if needed, identifying buyers in advance, and reviewing what worked and what didn't in the previous season. Start planning at least 2 months before the season begins.
response_tw: Bere siesie sɛ aka: hwɛ wo sika ne adenneɛ a wowɔ, paw nnuaba a wobɛkura wɔ aguabasa hia ne asase ho, lem adenneɛ dodo a wohia na tɔ ansa na bere aba, siesie obi a obɛyɛ adwuma wɔ bere a ɛhia dodo, siesie nsuo to sɛ ɛhia, hwehwɛ adeɛtɔfo ansa na bere aba, na hwɛ dea yɛ adwuma yie ne dea anyɛ yie wɔ bere a edi ɛkan. Fi siesie abosome 2 ansa na bere aba.

instruction_en: How do I deal with flooding on my farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mehwɛ nsuo a ɛhyɛ m'afuo mu?
response_en: If your farm floods regularly, take these steps: dig drainage channels around and through the field to direct water away, create raised beds to keep plant roots above waterline, plant flood-tolerant crop varieties, install water pumps to remove standing water quickly, build earthen bunds around the field perimeter, and consider planting floating rice varieties if flooding is very severe and frequent.
response_tw: Sɛ nsuo taa hye wo afuo mu a, yɛ eyi: hu mmɔnkyemmonkyem twa ne wɔ afuo mu de nsuo no kɔ, yɛ asase-sor afuo sɛ nnuaba ntini atena sor nsuo soronko, to aba a ɛtumi nsuo tenten ase hyɛ, si nsuo pumps sɛ yi nsuo a ɛda hɔ ntɛm, yɛ asase bɔ twa afuo no ho, na hwɛ fa floating rice aba sɛ nsuo tenten taa ba na ɛyɛ dodo.

instruction_en: How do I protect my farm from theft?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekora m'afuo fi korɔmfo ho?
response_en: Protect your farm by: building proper fencing with thorny hedges or wire fencing, using watchdogs especially at harvest time, forming community vigilance groups with neighboring farmers, harvesting promptly when crops are ready to minimize field time after ripening, installing basic alarm systems if affordable, registering your farm to establish legal ownership, and developing good relationships with neighboring community members.
response_tw: Kora wo afuo wɔ: si fense yie wɔ nkyerɛ nhwiren anaa wire, fa ɔkraman kora titiriw bere a wogye nnuaba, si kuo ne okuafo a wɔbɛn wo sɛ wɔhwɛ so, gye nnuaba ntɛm bere a ɛawie ase sɛ afuo mu bere sua akyi a nnuaba awi, si basic alarm sɛ sika wɔ ho, kyerɛw wo afuo din sɛ wunya asase krataa yie, na yɛ abakɔsɛm yie ne oman mu nnipa a wɔbɛn wo.

instruction_en: How do I make neem pesticide at home?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ neem adwummakers dawuro wɔ fie?
response_en: Crush 500g of neem seeds, soak in 10 litres of water overnight, strain through cloth, and add a little soap. Spray on crops in early morning or evening. Neem controls aphids, whiteflies, and caterpillars. Reapply after rain.
response_tw: Tutu neem aba 500g, da wɔ nsuo litre 10 mu anadwo koro, hun wɔ ntama mu, na de sapo kakra ka ho. Guare nnuaba so owia da ano anaa anwummere. Neem kora aphid, fitaa abowa, ne aborɔnoma ase yie. De to so bio nsuo akyi.
prompt: Question: How do I make neem pesticide at home?
completion: Crush 500g of neem seeds, soak in 10 litres of water overnight, strain through cloth, and add a little soap. Spray on crops in early morning or evening. Neem controls aphids, whiteflies, and caterpillars. Reapply after rain.
prompt_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ neem adwummakers dawuro wɔ fie?
completion_tw: Tutu neem aba 500g, da wɔ nsuo litre 10 mu anadwo koro, hun wɔ ntama mu, na de sapo kakra ka ho. Guare nnuaba so owia da ano anaa anwummere. Neem kora aphid, fitaa abowa, ne aborɔnoma ase yie. De to so bio nsuo akyi.

instruction_en: What is organic farming?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na nhwiren foforo okuafo adwuma yɛ?
response_en: Organic farming means growing crops without synthetic chemicals or fertilizers, using only natural inputs like compost, manure, neem, and biological pest controls. It improves soil health over time, is safer for consumers, and can command premium prices in some markets.
response_tw: Nhwiren Foforo Okuafo Adwuma kyerɛ sɛ wokura nnuaba a womfa chemical anaa ferefere ahemfie bi, na wode nhwiren foforo adenneɛ pɛ te sɛ compost, aguare, neem, ne nhwiren adwummakers kontrol. Ɛsiesie asase mu yiye bere akyi, ɛho tumi na adeɛtɔfo, na wobetumi tɔn boɔ kɛse wɔ aguabasa bi mu.

instruction_en: How does mulching help my farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mulching boa m'afuo?
response_en: Mulching covers soil with dry grass, straw, leaves, or plastic and conserves soil moisture reducing watering by 50%, regulates soil temperature, suppresses weed growth, and adds organic matter as it decomposes. Apply 5-10cm thick around plants.
response_tw: Mulching sɛɛ asase wɔ atɛntɛnni kraen, tow, nhaban, anaa plastik na ɛkora nsukuro wɔ asase mu ma nsuo to sua 50%, siesie asase osu, kora nhaban foforo ase, na de nhwiren foforo ka ho bere a ɛadwo. De sɛntimita 5-10 to nnuaba ho.

instruction_en: What plants can I grow to naturally repel pests?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Nnuaba bɛn na metumi kura sɛ wɔkora adwummakers ase nhwiren ho?
response_en: Plant marigolds, basil, garlic, or neem trees around your farm or between crop rows. These release compounds that deter many insects. Pyrethrum is very effective. Push-pull farming uses Desmodium grass between crop rows to repel stemborers.
response_tw: To marigold, basil, gyene kokoo, anaa neem nnua wo afuo ho anaa wɔ nnuaba ntam. Wɔn pue nhwiren a ɛtete adwummakers bebree. Pyrethrum yɛ adwuma pa ara. Push-pull farming de Desmodium atɛntɛnni to nnuaba ntam sɛ ɛkora stemborer ase yie pa ara.

instruction_en: What is composting and how do I make it?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na composting yɛ na ɛdeɛn na meyɛ no?
response_en: Composting is turning organic waste into natural fertilizer. Mix green materials like grass and vegetable waste with brown materials like dry leaves and straw in a pile. Keep it moist and turn it every 2 weeks. After 2-3 months you will have rich compost ready for your farm.
response_tw: Composting yɛ dan nhwiren foforo asennua yɛ nhwiren ferefere. Ka nhwiren foforo adeɛ te sɛ atɛntɛnni ne nnuan asennua ka bom ne nhwiren ɛda adeɛ te sɛ nhaban kraen ne tow wɔ bɔ koro. Ma ɛnte na tware no nnawotwe 2 biara. Abosome 2-3 akyi wobɛnya compost pa.

instruction_en: How do I make biochar to improve soil?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ biochar sɛ siesie asase?
response_en: Biochar is charcoal made from organic matter that improves soil when added. To make it: dig a pit or use a metal drum, fill with dry crop residues, wood chips, or husks, ignite and let it burn slowly with limited air (not fully exposed to oxygen), cover when partially charred to stop burning, let it cool, crush into small pieces, mix with compost before adding to soil. Biochar lasts in soil for hundreds of years and improves water and nutrient retention.
response_tw: Biochar yɛ efi a wɔyɛ fi nhwiren foforo a ɛsiesie asase bere a wɔde ka ho. Yɛ no eyi: hu tumtum anaa fa metal drum, hye nnuaba nsennua a ayaw, dua bi anaa nkuto mu, hyew na ma ɛhye brɛ brɛ a mframa ketewa wɔ mu (amfa oxygen mu pɛpɛɛpɛ), sɛɛ bere a ɛrehye kakra sɛ ɛnhye bio, ma ɛhuru, tutu yɛ mmerɛ, ka compost ho ansa na wode ka asase ho. Biochar tena asase mu mfe ahabiri na ɛsiesie nsuo ne aduan guina.

instruction_en: What is vermicomposting and how do I start?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na vermicomposting yɛ na ɛdeɛn na mesi ase?
response_en: Vermicomposting uses earthworms to break down organic waste into very high-quality compost called vermicompost or worm castings. To start: get a container or build a wooden box, add bedding of moist shredded newspaper or cardboard, add earthworms (red wigglers are best), feed with fruit and vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, and eggshells. Avoid meat, dairy, and oily food. Harvest the rich compost from the bottom every 2-3 months.
response_tw: Vermicomposting fa asase nwirɛntwi di dwuma sɛ pɛ nhwiren foforo asennua yɛ compost a ɛwɔ aduan pa pa ara a wɔfre vermicompost anaa worm castings. Sɛ wohwehwɛ si ase: nya nkuku anaa yɛ dua box, de krataa anaa cardboard a wɔatwa twa na wɔapo mu de yɛ mpa, de asase nwirɛntwi hyɛ (red wiggler yɛ pa pa ara), de nnuan ne aba asennua, coffee, ne kosua nkuku hyɛ. Bɔ hwɛ nam, nkyene aduan, ne ngo aduan. Gye compost a ɛwɔ ase hɔ no abosome 2-3 biara.

instruction_en: How do I control pests organically in a vegetable garden?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi kora adwummakers ase nhwiren ho wɔ nnuan afuo mu?
response_en: Organic pest control methods for vegetables include: handpicking large insects and caterpillars, using physical barriers like row covers or netting, companion planting with pest-repelling plants, spraying with homemade neem oil, garlic, or chili solutions, attracting beneficial insects with flowering plants, using kaolin clay sprays to confuse insects, and maintaining healthy soil and plants as the best long-term defense.
response_tw: Nhwiren adwummakers kora anammɔn ma nnuan ne: twe adwummakers kɛse ne aborɔnoma wɔ nsa, fa akwantempon a wɔde nnuaba so te sɛ row covers anaa net, ka nnuaba a wɔkora adwummakers ase ho, guare fie neem dawuro, gyene, anaa mako a wɔayɛ, twe adwummakers pa wɔ abode nnuaba ho, fa kaolin clay guare sɛ ɛbue adwummakers no, na kora asase ne nnuaba aho yie sɛ anammɔn pa ara wɔ bere tenten mu.

instruction_en: What is the push-pull farming system?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na push-pull farming systɛm yɛ?
response_en: Push-pull is a clever intercropping system developed in Africa that controls stemborers (the most destructive maize pest) without chemicals. Plant Desmodium grass between maize rows (it repels/pushes stemborers away) and Napier grass or Brachiaria around the field border (it attracts/pulls stemborers and traps them). Desmodium also fixes nitrogen and suppresses Striga weed. This system is free to maintain once established.
response_tw: Push-pull yɛ intercropping anammɔn ahorow a wɔyɛ wɔ Afrika a ɛkora stemborer (aburow adwummaker a ɛsɛe pa ara) ase a wɔmfa chemicals. To Desmodium atɛntɛnni wɔ aburow nnua ntam (ɛkora/push stemborer kɔ) na Napier atɛntɛnni anaa Brachiaria twa afuo no ho (ɛtwe/pull stemborer na gye wɔn). Desmodium nso fi nitrogen na kora Striga nhaban foforo ase. Anammɔn no yɛ daa akyi a wɔasi no yie.

instruction_en: Can I grow crops without any inputs at all?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Metumi kura nnuaba a menhwɛ ho saa ara anaa?
response_en: Growing without any external inputs is very difficult and usually leads to low yields. However, you can minimize purchased inputs by: making your own compost and manure, saving your own open-pollinated seeds, making homemade organic pesticides, using crop rotation to naturally restore soil fertility, and relying on rainwater. This approach requires more knowledge and management but can be cost-effective for subsistence farming.
response_tw: Kura a wonhwɛ ho adenneɛ biara yɛ den pa ara na taa ma nnuaba da sor sua. Nanso wobetumi sua adenneɛ a wotɔ wɔ: yɛ wo compost ne aguare ho, gye wo open-pollinated aba, yɛ fie nhwiren adwummakers dawuro, fa nnuaba sesae sɛ siesie asase ho nhwiren a enni sika, na fa osu nsuo. Anammɔn no hia nyansam ne hwɛ ase dodo nanso tumi yɛ adeɛ pa ma fie okuafo adwuma.

instruction_en: How do I reduce post-harvest losses?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi yɛ na m'aduan a megye no akyi anko mpoano?
response_en: Harvest at the right time, handle produce gently to avoid bruising, dry grains properly, store in appropriate conditions, transport in ventilated containers, and sell quickly for perishables. Post-harvest losses in Ghana can reach 30-40%.
response_tw: Gye nnuaba bere a ɛsɛ, de nnuaba no a eye yie sɛ ɛnhow, yaw nnuaba a ayaw no yie, guina wɔ bae a ɛsɛ, de kɔ bae a mframa tumi pue mu, na tɔn ntɛm sɛ ɛyɛ aduan a ɛsɛe ntɛm. Ghana mu aduan a ɛko aseɛ wɔ gye akyi betumi kɔ 30-40%.

instruction_en: How do I dry my grains properly after harvest?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyaw m'nnuaba yie akyi a megye wɔn?
response_en: Spread grains on clean tarpaulins or raised drying platforms in the sun. Stir regularly for even drying. Avoid drying on bare soil which introduces dirt and moisture. Grains are dry enough when a handful feels cool not warm and hard seeds do not dent easily.
response_tw: To nnuaba wɔ tarpaulin horo anaa platform a ɛda sor wɔ owia mu. Sane wɔn pɛ sɛ wɔyaw pɛ. Bɔ hwɛ yaw wɔ asase firi so efisɛ ɛde fufuo ne nsuo ba mu. Nnuaba yaw yie bere a wo nsa a wode fa no yɛ huru na aba den nyi wɔ nsateaa ase ntɛm.

instruction_en: My harvested tomatoes are rotting quickly. What can I do?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: M'ntomato a megye wɔn resɛe ntɛm. Dɛn na metumi yɛ?
response_en: Avoid harvesting during wet conditions. Handle gently to prevent bruising. Store in cool shaded well-ventilated areas on raised surfaces. Remove and separate any rotting fruits immediately. Consider making tomato paste or drying to reduce losses. Transport in ventilated crates not plastic bags.
response_tw: Bɔ hwɛ gye bere a nsuo wɔ hɔ. De wɔn pɛ sɛ wɔnhow. Guina wɔ bae a ɛhuru, owia ntra mu, mframa pue mu na ɛda sor. Yi na twa ntomato a resɛe ntɛm ara. Fa tomato paste yɛ anaa yaw sɛ mpampa sika kɔ mpoano.

instruction_en: How do I reduce post-harvest losses for vegetables?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi sua nnuan a megye wɔn akyi mpoano?
response_en: Harvest vegetables early in the morning when cool, handle gently, sort out damaged ones immediately, store in cool ventilated areas, and sell quickly. For leafy vegetables, wrap in damp cloth to extend freshness. Avoid long transport in hot conditions.
response_tw: Gye nnuan owia da ano bere a ɛhuru, de wɔn pɛ, yi a wɔasɛe ntɛm ara, guina wɔ bae a ɛhuru na mframa pue mu, na tɔn ntɛm. Nhaban nnuan ho, de ntama nte de sɛɛ wɔn sɛ ɛma wɔn tena pɛ.

instruction_en: What is grading and why is it important for selling produce?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na grading yɛ na ɛsɛ sɛ ɛho hia sɛ wotɔn nnuaba?
response_en: Grading means sorting produce into quality categories based on size, color, shape, and freedom from defects. It is important because: buyers pay premium prices for Grade A produce, graded produce has longer shelf life when sorted, it builds trust with buyers and processors, it allows you to sell different grades to different markets, and it makes it easier to negotiate better prices. Grade A goes to supermarkets, Grade B to restaurants, Grade C to processors.
response_tw: Grading kyerɛ yi nnuaba no mu wɔ aba ho pa ahodoɔ wɔ boɔ, rere, ahofã, ne mfomso a enni ho so. Ɛho hia efisɛ: adeɛtɔfo tua sika dodo ma Grade A nnuaba, nnuaba a wɔasi no mu tena yie bere tenten, ɛkyerɛ adeɛtɔfo ne adwuma kuo sɛ wobetumi de wɔn so, ɛma wo tumi tɔn Grade ahodoɔ wɔ aguabasa ahodoɔ, na ɛboa wo ne adeɛtɔfo siesie boɔ yie. Grade A kɔ supermarkets, Grade B kɔ adidibea, Grade C kɔ adwuma kuo.

Grade A goes to supermarkets, Grade B to restaurants, Grade C to processors.

completion_tw: Grading kyerɛ yi nnuaba no mu wɔ aba ho pa ahodoɔ wɔ boɔ, rere, ahofã, ne mfomso a enni ho so. Ɛho hia efisɛ: adeɛtɔfo tua sika dodo ma Grade A nnuaba, nnuaba a wɔasi no mu tena yie bere tenten, ɛkyerɛ adeɛtɔfo ne adwuma kuo sɛ wobetumi de wɔn so, ɛma wo tumi tɔn Grade ahodoɔ wɔ aguabasa ahodoɔ, na ɛboa wo ne adeɛtɔfo siesie boɔ yie. Grade A kɔ supermarkets, Grade B kɔ adidibea, Grade C kɔ adwuma kuo.

instruction_en: How do I make dried vegetable products to extend shelf life?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ nnuan a ayaw sɛ wɔtena yie bere tenten?
response_en: Dry vegetables by: washing and slicing thinly, blanching (briefly boiling) leafy vegetables for 1-2 minutes before drying to preserve color and nutrients, spreading on clean raised drying racks in the sun, or using a solar dryer. Vegetables are dry when crisp and hard — usually 2-5 days in sun. Pack in sealed airtight bags and store in a cool dry place. Dried vegetables last 6-12 months and can be sold as a higher-value product.
response_tw: Yaw nnuan wɔ: hohoro na twa twa yie, blanch (hyew kakra) nhaban nnuan sɛntɔ 1-2 ansa na woyaw sɛ ɛkora wɔn rere ne aduan ho nhwiren, de to drying rack horo a ɛda sor wɔ owia mu, anaa fa solar dryer. Nnuan yaw wie bere a wɔyɛ den na wɔpae — taa nnafoa 2-5 wɔ owia mu. Paka wɔ bags a apo so na guina wɔ bae a ɛhuru ne yera. Nnuan a ayaw tena abosome 6-12 na wobetumi tɔn wɔ boɔ kɛse.

instruction_en: How do I make peanut butter from groundnuts?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ nkatie betere fi nkatie mu?
response_en: To make peanut butter: sort and remove damaged nuts, roast in a pan on low heat until golden brown (20-30 minutes), stir constantly to prevent burning, remove skins while warm, grind in a blender or milling machine until smooth, add salt to taste, and pack in clean sealed containers. Can add a little vegetable oil if too thick. Homemade peanut butter sells well and has a 2-3 month shelf life at room temperature.
response_tw: Yɛ nkatie betere eyi: yi na si nnuaba a asɛe, hyew wɔ pan so ogya sua mu kosi sɛ ɛdan hoho bɔɔ (sɛntɔ 20-30), sane pɛ sɛ ɛnhyew, yi nkuto a ɛwɔ so bere a ɛhuru, blend wɔ blender anaa mill mu kosi sɛ ɛyɛ mmerɛ, de ɛwa ka ho sɛ dɛm, na paka wɔ nkuku horo a apo so. Tumi de vegetable dawuro kakra ka ho sɛ ɛhwa pa dodo. Fie nkatie betere tɔn yie na ɛtena abosome 2-3 wɔ fie osu mu.

Can add a little vegetable oil if too thick. Homemade peanut butter sells well and has a 2-3 month shelf life at room temperature.

instruction_en: How do I make palm oil at home?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ ngo wɔ fie?
response_en: To process palm oil at home: harvest fresh bunches and ferment for 3-5 days to loosen fruits, thresh fruits from the bunch, boil fruits for 30-60 minutes until soft, pound or press to separate oil from fiber, add water and boil the pulp again, skim oil from the surface, filter through cloth, boil the filtered oil to remove water (until bubbling stops), and cool before storing in sealed containers. Traditional palm press improves extraction efficiency.
response_tw: Yɛ ngo wɔ fie eyi: gye bɔ foforo na ma wɔn da nnafoa 3-5 sɛ aba no pae yie, gye aba fi bɔ no ho, hyew aba no sɛntɔ 30-60 kosi sɛ wɔyɛ mmerɛ, tutu anaa pɛm sɛ yi ngo fi nsennua mu, de nsuo ka aba no ka ho na hyew bio, yi ngo fi so, hun wɔ ntama mu, hyew ngo a wɔahun no sɛ yi nsuo (kosi sɛ bubble ahwie), na ma ɛhuru ansa na waguina wɔ nkuku a apo so. Traditional palm press boa sɛ wunya ngo dodo.

Traditional palm press improves extraction efficiency.

instruction_en: How do I add value to cassava to increase my income?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mede boɔ ka bankye ho sɛ menya sika dodo?
response_en: Cassava value-added products include: gari (fermented and roasted cassava), fufu flour, cassava flour for baking, cassava chips (dried slices), cassava starch for industrial use, kokonte (dried cassava), and high-quality fufu. Each step of processing adds value and income. Gari is especially popular and can double the income from fresh cassava. Consider forming a women's group to process together to reduce costs and increase volumes.
response_tw: Bankye ho adwuma a wɔde yɛ dodo ne: gari (bankye a wɔade na hyew), fufu mfurow, bankye mfurow ma bobe adwuma, bankye chips (slices a ayaw), bankye starch ma adwuma kuro, kokonte (bankye a ayaw), ne fufu a ɛwɔ aho pa. Adwuma biara a wɔde yɛ de boɔ ne sika ka ho. Gari pɛ pa ara na ɛtumi ma bankye foforo sika abien. Hwɛ si mmaa kuo sɛ wɔyɛ adwuma bom sɛ sua sika ne ma nnuaba dodo ba.

instruction_en: How do I grow pepper successfully?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura mako yie?
response_en: Pepper grows best in warm sunny conditions with well-drained fertile soil. Start seeds in a nursery then transplant 4-6 week old seedlings. Space 45-60 cm apart. Water regularly, stake tall plants, and apply balanced fertilizer.
response_tw: Mako da sor pa wɔ owia mu na asase a nsuo pue mu yie na ɛwɔ aduan pa. Fi aba fi nursery, afei si nnuaba a wɔdin nnawotwe 4-6 wɔ wɔ beae foforɔ. Gyae asukyerɛ sɛntimita 45-60. De nsuo to so pɛ, si aduro nnua a wɔreden, na de ferefere pɛ to so.

instruction_en: How long does it take for cowpea to mature?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Bere ahe na ɛsɛ ma abɔdweɛ awie ase?
response_en: Cowpea matures in 60-90 days depending on the variety. Harvest when pods turn yellow-brown and dry. For fresh green beans, harvest when pods are full but still green about 60 days after planting.
response_tw: Abɔdweɛ wie ase wɔ nnafoa 60-90 mu wɔ aba no ahodoɔ so. Gye bere a fidie no dan sika ɛda na ayera. Abɔdweɛ foforo ho, gye bere a fidie no ayɛ ma nanso ɛda koraa nnafoa 60 akyi a wode to mu.

instruction_en: How do I grow onions in the dry season?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura gyene wɔ ɔpɛ bere mu?
response_en: Onions grow best in cool dry conditions. Plant sets or transplants in the dry season November to February in northern Ghana. Irrigate regularly. Harvest when tops fall over naturally. Dry in shade before storage.
response_tw: Gyene da sor pa wɔ huru tirim na ɛyera. To sets anaa transplants wɔ ɔpɛ bere mu. De nsuo to so pɛ. Gye bere a wɔn tenten atu. Yaw wɔ owia sum ansa na waguina.

instruction_en: How do I grow watermelon successfully?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura watermelon yie?
response_en: Watermelon needs well-drained sandy loam soil, warm temperatures, and plenty of sunlight. Space 1.5-2 m apart. Water deeply but infrequently. Apply potassium-rich fertilizer during fruiting. Harvest when the tendril near the fruit turns brown.
response_tw: Watermelon hia asintiɛ asase a nsuo pue mu yie, owia mu, na owia hye pa. Gyae asukyerɛ mita 1.5-2. De nsuo to asase mu yiye nanso mmere pii bi. De potassium ferefere to so bere a aba reba. Gye bere a ntini a ɛbɛn aba no dan ɛda.

instruction_en: How do I increase the yield of my cocoa farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi ma m'kookoo da sor dodo?
response_en: Prune cocoa trees regularly to remove dead wood and improve light. Apply recommended fertilizers. Control black pod disease by removing infected pods and spraying copper-based fungicides. Replace unproductive old trees with improved varieties.
response_tw: Twa kookoo nnua so pɛ sɛ yi dua kum ne nnua a wɔawu fi mu na ma owia hyɛ mu. De ferefere a wɔaka to so. Kora black pod yadeɛ ase wɔ yi pods a wɔayarɛ na de copper fungicide guare so. Si nnua kedee a wonnya bi fi wɔn ho na de aba foforɔ a ɛda sor wɔ wɔn beae.

instruction_en: What causes cocoa black pod disease and how do I control it?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na ɛma kookoo black pod yadeɛ na ɛdeɛn na metumi kora ase?
response_en: Black pod is caused by Phytophthora fungus and spreads in wet humid conditions. It turns pods black and destroys beans. Control by removing and burying infected pods, pruning trees for better air circulation, and spraying copper-based fungicides regularly during rainy season.
response_tw: Black pod fi Phytophthora fungus na ɛtete wɔ nsuo ne huhu mu. Ɛdan pods tuntum na ɛsɛe aba no. Kora ase wɔ yi ne sisie pods a wɔayarɛ, twa nnua so sɛ mframa pue mu yie, na de copper fungicide guare pɛ wɔ osu bere mu.

instruction_en: How do I grow ginger successfully?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura kakaduro yie?
response_en: Ginger grows best in partially shaded humid conditions with rich well-drained soil. Plant rhizomes 5-7 cm deep spaced 20-30 cm apart. Keep soil moist and mulch heavily. Harvest after 8-10 months when leaves yellow and die back.
response_tw: Kakaduro da sor pa wɔ beae a owia tra mu kakra na ɛwɔ huhu, asase a ɛwɔ aduan pa na nsuo pue mu yie. De rhizomes to asase mu sɛntimita 5-7 na gyae asukyerɛ sɛntimita 20-30. Kora asase nte na de mulch to so papaapa. Gye abosome 8-10 akyi a nhaban no dan sika na awu.

instruction_en: When is the right time to harvest sorghum?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Bere bɛn na ɛsɛ sɛ megye ɔtɛ?
response_en: Harvest sorghum when grain is hard, heads have turned their mature color, and moisture content is around 20-25%. Dry after harvest before storage. Harvest promptly to avoid bird damage.
response_tw: Gye ɔtɛ bere a aba no ayɛ den, aba no dinn adan wɔn rere na nsukuro yɛ bɛn 20-25%. Yaw akyi a wogye ansa na waguina. Gye ntɛm sɛ anomaa mfomso anba.

instruction_en: What fertilizer is good for plantain?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ferefere bɛn na ɛyɛ ɔkorɔ ma borɔdɔ?
response_en: Plantain responds well to organic matter such as manure and compost plus potassium-rich fertilizers. Apply NPK 15-15-15 at planting then top-dress with muriate of potash during bunch development. Potassium is critical for good bunch weight and disease resistance.
response_tw: Borɔdɔ gye nhwiren foforo aduan pa te sɛ aguare ne compost a potassium ferefere ka ho yiye. De NPK 15-15-15 to mu bere a wode to mu afei de muriate of potash to so bere a aba reba. Potassium ho hia pa ara ma bɔ kɛse ne yadeɛ tumi.

instruction_en: How do I control Panama disease in plantain and banana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi kora Panama yadeɛ ase wɔ borɔdɔ ne kwadu mu?
response_en: Panama disease is a fatal fungal soil disease. Use certified disease-free planting material, avoid planting in previously infected soil, practice strict hygiene by cleaning tools before moving between farms, and plant resistant varieties like Cardaba or FHIA hybrids. There is no chemical cure.
response_tw: Panama yadeɛ yɛ fungal asase yadeɛ a ɛkum. De aba a wɔakyerɛw wɔ ho a enni yadeɛ to mu, bɔ hwɛ to wɔ asase a yadeɛ aka ho ansa, yɛ ho tumi yie wɔ hohoro adeɛ ansa na wosesa afuo, na to aba a ɛtumi hyɛ ase te sɛ Cardaba anaa FHIA.

completion: Panama disease is a fatal fungal soil disease. There is no chemical cure.

instruction_en: How do I grow plantain successfully?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura borɔdɔ yie?
response_en: Plant plantain suckers from healthy mother plants. Choose planting material free from pests. Dig holes 60cm x 60cm x 60cm and fill with compost. Space plants 3m x 3m. Mulch heavily, water in dry periods, and apply potassium-rich fertilizer for good bunch development.
response_tw: To borɔdɔ aba fi nnua a wɔwɔ aho na wɔsom yie. Paw aba a enni adwummakers. Hu tumtum sɛntimita 60 x 60 x 60 na hye compost mu. Gyae asukyerɛ mita 3 x 3. De mulch to so papaapa, de nsuo to ɔpɛ bere mu, na de potassium ferefere to sɛ aba reba yie.

instruction_en: What variety of rice grows best in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɔmɔ aba bɛn na ɛda sor pa wɔ Ghana?
response_en: Popular rice varieties in Ghana include AGRA Rice, Jasmine 85, and NERICA varieties. NERICA varieties are resistant to drought and local pests. Contact your local MOFA extension officer for variety recommendations suited to your specific area.
response_tw: Ɔmɔ aba a wɔpɛ wɔ Ghana ho ne AGRA Rice, Jasmine 85, ne NERICA aba. NERICA aba tumi nsuo pa ne beae adwummakers ase hyɛ. Ka kyerɛ wo MOFA extension officer a ɛbɛn wo sɛ ɔka aba a ɛfata wo beae ho.

instruction_en: How do I grow cashew successfully?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura cashew yie?
response_en: Cashew grows best in well-drained sandy or laterite soils in areas with a distinct dry season. Plant seeds directly 5 cm deep in the field or transplant seedlings. Space trees 8-10 meters apart. Apply fertilizer in the second year onward. Control collar rot disease, cashew stem and root borer insects, and anthracnose. Harvest nuts when the cashew apple turns yellow or red and the nut is dark grey. Cashew trees start producing at 3-5 years.
response_tw: Cashew da sor pa wɔ asase a nsuo pue mu yie a ɛyɛ asintiɛ anaa laterite wɔ beae a ɔpɛ bere wɔ hɔ. To aba tɔtɔ wɔ asase mu sɛntimita 5 anaa si nnuaba mfoforo ananmu. Gyae nnua asukyerɛ mita 8-10. De ferefere to mfe a ɛto so abien ananmu. Kora collar rot yadeɛ, cashew tibo ne ntini adwummakers, ne anthracnose ase. Gye aba bere a cashew apple no dan hoho anaa kɔkɔɔ na aba no dan tuntum-brɔdwa. Cashew nnua da sor fi mfe 3-5 akyi.

instruction_en: How do I grow soybeans profitably?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura soya yɛ adwuma sɛ menya sika?
response_en: Soybeans are highly profitable legumes. Plant after rains are well established. Inoculate seeds with Bradyrhizobium bacteria before planting (this fixes nitrogen and can triple yields). Space 30-45 cm within rows and 60 cm between rows. Apply phosphorus fertilizer at planting. Weed once at 3 weeks. Harvest when 95% of pods have turned yellow-brown. Dry to 12% moisture before storage. Soybeans have strong demand from oil processors and animal feed manufacturers.
response_tw: Soya yɛ abɔdweɛ a ɛde sika ba pa ara. To akyi a osu asi yie. De Bradyrhizobium bacteria ka aba ho ansa na woto (eyi fi nitrogen na ɛtumi ma nnuaba da sor mprɛ mmiɛnsa). Gyae asukyerɛ sɛntimita 30-45 wɔ ntam ne sɛntimita 60 wɔ nnua so ntam. De phosphorus ferefere to mu bere a wode to. Tia nhaban foforo bere a ɛto so mmiɛnsa no nnawotwe baako. Gye bere a 95% fidie no adan hoho bɔɔ. Yaw kosi 12% nsuku ansa na waguina. Soya hia yɛ kɛse wɔ ngo adwuma kuo ne mmoa aduan adwuma kuo ho.

instruction_en: How do I grow sweet potato successfully?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura asikyire bankye yie?
response_en: Sweet potato grows best in well-drained sandy loam soil. Plant vine cuttings (30-40 cm long) not seeds, inserting 2/3 of the cutting into the soil. Space 30 cm apart on ridges 90 cm apart. Apply NPK at planting. Weed early. Harvest 3-5 months after planting when leaves begin to yellow. Sweet potato is drought-tolerant and can grow in poor soils — excellent for climate resilience. The leaves are also nutritious and can be eaten as vegetables.
response_tw: Asikyire bankye da sor pa wɔ asase a nsuo pue mu yie na ɛyɛ asintiɛ loam. To vine cuttings (sɛntimita 30-40 tenten) na mfa aba, de 2/3 wɔ cutting no to asase mu. Gyae asukyerɛ sɛntimita 30 wɔ ntwaho so a ɛgyae sɛntimita 90. De NPK to mu bere a wode to. Tia nhaban foforo ntɛm. Gye abosome 3-5 akyi a wode to mu bere a nhaban reredan hoho. Asikyire bankye tumi nsuo pa ase hyɛ na ɛtumi da sor wɔ asase a enni pa — ɛyɛ ɔkorɔ pa ara ma katawere mu sɛsae ho. Nhaban no nso wɔ aduan ho nhwiren ne wobetumi di sɛ nnuan.

instruction_en: How do I grow pineapple commercially?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura aborɔfo wɔ adwuma mu?
response_en: Commercial pineapple production: plant suckers or crowns from healthy plants, space 60x30 cm on raised beds in rows, apply NPK fertilizer 3 times per year, force flowering by applying ethephon or acetylene solution at 12-14 months to synchronize harvest, control mealy bugs and heart rot disease, harvest 15-18 months after planting. MD2 and Smooth Cayenne are popular export varieties. Ghana exports significant volumes of pineapple to Europe.
response_tw: Aborɔfo adwuma kura ho: to suckers anaa crowns fi nnua a wɔwɔ aho wɔ, gyae asukyerɛ sɛntimita 60x30 wɔ asase-sor afuo wɔ nnua so mu, de NPK ferefere to mprɛ mmiɛnsa bere biara, ma abode ba wɔ de ethephon anaa acetylene solution hyɛ wɔ bosome 12-14 akyi sɛ wogye bere koro, kora mealy bug ne heart rot yadeɛ ase, gye bosome 15-18 akyi a wode to mu. MD2 ne Smooth Cayenne yɛ export aba a wɔpɛ wɔn. Ghana de aborɔfo kɔ Yurop dodo.

MD2 and Smooth Cayenne are popular export varieties. Ghana exports significant volumes of pineapple to Europe.

instruction_en: How do I grow moringa and what are its benefits?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekura moringa na mfaaso bɛn na ɛwɔ?
response_en: Moringa is one of the most nutritious plants in the world and grows easily in Ghana. Plant seeds directly or use stem cuttings in well-drained soil. It tolerates drought and poor soils. Harvest leaves regularly to keep the tree short and productive. Moringa leaves can be eaten fresh, dried, and powdered. Benefits include: extremely high in iron, vitamins A and C, protein, and calcium. It is used medicinally and sold as a nutritional supplement at premium prices.
response_tw: Moringa yɛ nnua a ɛwɔ aduan ho nhwiren pa ara wɔ wiase mu na ɛda sor yie wɔ Ghana. To aba tɔtɔ anaa fa tibo cutting wɔ asase a nsuo pue mu yie. Ɛtumi nsuo pa ne asase a enni pa ase hyɛ. Gye nhaban pɛ sɛ ɛkora dua no tia ne adwuma mu. Moringa nhaban wobetumi di foforo, yaw na tutu. Mfaaso ho ne: iron, vitamins A ne C, protein, ne calcium ho yɛ dodo pa ara. Wɔde di dawuro adwuma na tɔn sɛ nutritional supplement wɔ boɔ kɛse.

instruction_en: How do I control diseases in my cocoa nursery?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi kora yadeɛ ase wɔ m'kookoo nursery mu?
response_en: Common cocoa nursery diseases include: collar rot (Phytophthora), damping off, and black pod. Control by: using sterilized potting mix, ensuring good drainage in nursery bags, avoiding overwatering, removing and destroying diseased seedlings immediately, maintaining good spacing for air circulation, spraying with copper-based fungicides preventatively, and ensuring nursery shade cloth allows 50% light penetration.
response_tw: Kookoo nursery yadeɛ a wɔtaa hu ne: collar rot (Phytophthora), nnuaba-mfoforo wu yadeɛ, ne black pod. Kora ase wɔ: fa potting mix a wɔasi amu, kora nsuo pue mu yie wɔ nursery bags, bɔ hwɛ nsuo dodo, yi ne seaa nnuaba mfoforo a wɔayarɛ ntɛm, kora asukyerɛ yie sɛ mframa pue mu, guare wɔ copper-based fungicide sɛ kora ase ansa na yadeɛ reba, na kora sɛ nursery shade cloth ma owia 50% pue mu.

instruction_en: What is the best way to grow garden eggs (African eggplant)?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Anammɔn bɛn na ɛyɛ pa wɔ kura garden egg (Afrika ntrɔba)?
response_en: Garden eggs grow best in warm conditions with well-drained fertile soil. Start seeds in a nursery and transplant 4-6 week old seedlings. Space 60x60 cm. Apply NPK at planting and top-dress with urea at 4 weeks. Stake plants when they get large. Watch for mites, aphids, and flea beetles. Harvest when fruits are still firm and glossy — over-ripe fruits become bitter. Garden eggs are popular in Ghanaian cuisine and have strong local market demand.
response_tw: Garden egg da sor pa wɔ ahuhuro mu na asase a nsuo pue mu yie ne aduan pa. Fi aba fi nursery na si nnuaba mfoforo a wɔdin nnawotwe 4-6 ananmu. Gyae asukyerɛ sɛntimita 60x60. De NPK to mu bere a wode to na de urea to so nnawotwe 4 akyi. Si aduro nnua no bere a wɔreyɛ kɛse. Hwɛ mite, aphid, ne flea beetle ho. Gye bere a aba no da den na hyerɛn — aba a ɛawi dodo yɛ dɛm den. Garden egg pɛ pa ara wɔ Ghana ɔman mu aduan ho na ɛwɔ aguabasa hia kɛse.

instruction_en: How do I write a simple farm business plan?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ afuo adwuma dwumadie plan ketewa?
response_en: A simple farm business plan includes what you will grow and how much, land available, inputs needed and their costs, expected yield and selling price, total expected income, total costs, and expected profit. Also include risks and how you will manage them.
response_tw: Afuo adwuma dwumadie plan ketewa mu wɔ: dea wobɛkura ne sɛn, asase a wowɔ, adenneɛ a hia ne wɔn boɔ, nnuaba a wobɛnya ne tɔn boɔ, sika nyinaa a wobɛnya, sika nyinaa a wode, ne mfaaso a wobɛnya. Ka nsɔhwɛ ne ɛdeɛn na wobɛyɛ sɛ wobɛhwɛ won ase.

instruction_en: How do I find buyers for my farm produce before harvest?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mehwehwɛ adeɛtɔfo ma m'afuo nnuaba ansa na megye wɔn?
response_en: Visit local markets to identify traders. Contact food processing companies and restaurants. Use phone or WhatsApp groups for farmers in your area. Signing contract farming agreements with companies before the season guarantees buyers and sometimes provides inputs on credit.
response_tw: Kɔ aguabasa a ɛbɛn wo sɛ wuhu nnipa a wɔtɔn. Ka kyerɛ aduan yɛ adeɛ kuo ne adidibea. Fa fɔn anaa WhatsApp kuo ma okuafo wɔ wo beae. De contract farming agreement ka adeɛtɔ kuo bere a bere mfiase ansa na ɛma adeɛtɔfo yɛ dada.

completion: Visit local markets to identify traders. Contact food processing companies and restaurants. Use phone or WhatsApp groups for farmers in your area. Signing contract farming agreements with companies before the season guarantees buyers and sometimes provides inputs on credit.

instruction_en: What is contract farming and how does it benefit me?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na contract farming yɛ na ɛboa me sɛn?
response_en: Contract farming is an agreement between a farmer and a buyer before the season starts. The buyer agrees to purchase your produce at a fixed price after harvest. Benefits include guaranteed market, sometimes free inputs on credit, and technical support.
response_tw: Contract farming yɛ apam a ɛda okuafo ne adeɛtɔ kuo ntam ansa na bere no aba. Adeɛtɔ kuo no ka ho sɛ wɔbɛtɔ wo nnuaba boɔ a wɔaka akyi a wogye. Mfaaso ho ne: aguabasa a wɔadaa ho, adenneɛ a wɔde brɛ wo ka mu mmere bi, ne nnwuma boa.

instruction_en: What advice would you give to a young person starting farming for the first time?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Adesua bɛn na wobɛma aberantemmaa a ɔresi okuafo adwuma ɛkan?
response_en: Start small and learn as you grow. Choose one or two crops you understand well for your first season. Keep records of everything. Seek advice from experienced farmers and extension officers. Join a farmer group or cooperative. Do not give up after one bad season. Farming is a dignified and profitable business when done well.
response_tw: Fi ketewa na sua bere a woda sor. Paw nnuaba baako anaa abien a wohu wɔn yie bere a ɛdi ɛkan. Kora nsɛm nyinaa. Hwehwɛ nnwuma boa fi okuafo a wɔnim adwuma no ne extension officers. Ba okuafo kuo mu. Mma bere koro a aannya yie no amma wo anhuri. Okuafo adwuma yɛ adwuma a ɛwɔ anuonyam na ɛde sika ba sɛ woyɛ no yie.

instruction_en: Do I need to register my farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛsɛ sɛ mede m'afuo nkyerɛw?
response_en: Registering your farm with MOFA gives you access to government subsidies, training, extension services, credit, and market linkages. Visit your local MOFA office or District Assembly with your ID and land documents. Registration is free and very beneficial.
response_tw: Wo afuo nkyerɛw wɔ MOFA de tumi ma wo sɛ wunya ɔman ho boa, nyansapɔ, extension nnwuma, ka, ne aguabasa ho kyɛfa. Kɔ wo MOFA ofisi anaa District Assembly a ɛbɛn wo wɔ wo nkyerɛwsɛm ne asase krataa. Nkyerɛw yɛ daa na ɛboa pa ara.

completion: Registering your farm with MOFA gives you access to government subsidies, training, extension services, credit, and market linkages. Visit your local MOFA office or District Assembly with your ID and land documents. Registration is free and very beneficial.
prompt_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛsɛ sɛ mede m'afuo nkyerɛw?
completion_tw: Wo afuo nkyerɛw wɔ MOFA de tumi ma wo sɛ wunya ɔman ho boa, nyansapɔ, extension nnwuma, ka, ne aguabasa ho kyɛfa. Kɔ wo MOFA ofisi anaa District Assembly a ɛbɛn wo wɔ wo nkyerɛwsɛm ne asase krataa. Nkyerɛw yɛ daa na ɛboa pa ara.

instruction_en: How do I get certified to sell organic produce?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na menya kratafa sɛ metɔn organic nnuaba?
response_en: To sell as certified organic you need to farm organically for at least 3 years and pass inspection by an accredited certification body. In Ghana organizations like ENCOT and Control Union offer organic certification.
response_tw: Sɛ wohwehwɛ tɔn organic nnuaba a wɔakyerɛw wɔn a, ɛsɛ sɛ woyɛ organic okuafo adwuma mfe 3 pɛ na wufa inspection a organic kuo yɛ. Wɔ Ghana, kuo te sɛ ENCOT ne Control Union de organic kratafa brɛ.

instruction_en: What is GlobalGAP certification and how does it help farmers export?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na GlobalGAP kratafa yɛ na ɛboa okuafo sɛ wɔde nnuaba kɔ amanaman?
response_en: GlobalGAP (Good Agricultural Practices) is an international certification standard that verifies produce is grown safely and sustainably. It is required by most European supermarkets to import fresh produce. Getting certified opens doors to premium export markets. Requirements include: safe pesticide use with records, worker welfare, environmental protection, and food safety. In Ghana, groups like PFAG (Pesticide and Fertilizer Association of Ghana) can help farmers get certified.
response_tw: GlobalGAP (Good Agricultural Practices) yɛ amanaman kratafa nhyɛso a ɛkyerɛ sɛ nnuaba kura yie na ɛho tumi. Yurop supermarkets bebree hia sɛ na wɔde nnuaba foforo ba. Nya kratafa no bue aguabasa kɛse a wɔtɔn wɔn boɔ kɛse. Nhyɛso ho ne: adwummakers dawuro fa a ɛho tumi ne nsɛm kora, adwumafo ho hwɛ, ɛwim ho hwɛ, ne aduan ho tumi. Wɔ Ghana, kuo te sɛ PFAG (Pesticide and Fertilizer Association of Ghana) tumi boa okuafo nya kratafa.

instruction_en: How do I register my farm as a business in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mede m'afuo kyerɛw sɛ adwuma wɔ Ghana?
response_en: To register your farm as a business in Ghana: register with the Registrar General's Department as a sole proprietorship or partnership (costs GHS 150-300), get a Tax Identification Number (TIN) from the Ghana Revenue Authority, register with MOFA for agricultural activities, open a business bank account, and get all required operating permits from your local assembly. A formally registered farm business is more credible to banks, buyers, and government programs.
response_tw: Sɛ wohwehwɛ kyerɛw wo afuo sɛ adwuma wɔ Ghana a: kyerɛw wɔ Registrar General's Department sɛ sole proprietorship anaa partnership (sika GHS 150-300), nya Tax Identification Number (TIN) fi Ghana Revenue Authority hɔ, kyerɛw wɔ MOFA ho ma okuafo adwuma, bue adwuma ban account, na nya kratafa biara a wohia fi wo local assembly. Afuo adwuma a wɔakyerɛw no yɛ pa ara wɔ ban, adeɛtɔfo, ne ɔman adwuma ho.

A formally registered farm business is more credible to banks, buyers, and government programs.

instruction_en: What is the Fairtrade certification and what are its benefits for farmers?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na Fairtrade kratafa yɛ na mfaaso bɛn na ɛwɔ ho ma okuafo?
response_en: Fairtrade certification guarantees farmers a minimum price for their produce that covers production costs plus a social premium that is invested in community development. Benefits include: stable income even when world prices drop, Fairtrade premium funds for schools, health, and infrastructure, access to pre-financing before harvest, better trading relationships, and marketing support. Fairtrade is common in Ghana for cocoa, coffee, and fresh fruit. Contact Fairtrade Africa for information.
response_tw: Fairtrade kratafa hɔ kura sɛ okuafo nya boɔ a ɛdata wɔn nnuaba a ɛkata adwuma ho sika ne social premium a wɔde fa oman nkosuo mu. Mfaaso ho ne: sika a ɛda pɛ bere a amanaman boɔ tow, Fairtrade premium sika ma sukuu, yadeɛ, ne nkosuo adwuma, nya sika ansa na wogye nnuaba, adwuma ho apam a ɛyɛ pa, ne adwuma ho boa. Fairtrade pɛ wɔ Ghana ho ma kookoo, kɔfi, ne aba foforo. Ka kyerɛ Fairtrade Africa sɛ wunya nsɛm.

instruction_en: How do I get a license to sell agricultural chemicals in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na menya kratafa sɛ metɔn okuafo adwuma chemicals wɔ Ghana?
response_en: To sell agricultural chemicals (pesticides, fertilizers) in Ghana, you need: a license from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), which requires completing a training course on pesticide handling, passing an examination, paying the licensing fee, having appropriate storage facilities, and renewing the license annually. Selling agricultural chemicals without a license is illegal and carries penalties. Contact the EPA Pesticides Control and Management Centre for the application process.
response_tw: Sɛ wohwehwɛ tɔn okuafo adwuma chemicals (adwumakers dawuro, ferefere) wɔ Ghana a, wohia: kratafa fi Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), a ɛhia sɛ wo siesie adwummakers dawuro hwɛ suahu, fa exam mu, tua kratafa sika, wɔ guina fie a ɛfata, na sane kratafa mfe biara. Tɔn okuafo adwuma chemicals a wonni kratafa yɛ illegal na ɛwɔ akyinnye. Ka kyerɛ EPA Pesticides Control and Management Centre sɛ wuhu bisa anammɔn.

instruction_en: What records must I keep to qualify for agricultural loans?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Nsɛm bɛn na ɛsɛ mekora sɛ menya okuafo adwuma ka?
response_en: To qualify for agricultural loans, banks and microfinance institutions typically require: farm records showing area farmed and crops grown, sales records for at least one season, input purchase receipts, a farm business plan, MOFA registration certificate, land ownership or lease documents, a valid ID, and sometimes a guarantor. Start keeping records now even if you are not currently seeking a loan — it takes time to build a credible financial history that banks trust.
response_tw: Sɛ wohwehwɛ nya okuafo adwuma ka a, ban ne microfinance institutions taa hia: afuo nsɛm a ɛkyerɛ asase asukyerɛ ne nnuaba a wokura, tɔn nsɛm ma bere baako pɛ pɛ, adenneɛ tɔn krataa, afuo adwuma dwumadie plan, MOFA kyerɛwsɛm kratafa, asase wura anaa asɛ krataa, ID a ɛtia, na mmere bi obi a ɔbɛka ho. Fi kora nsɛm saa ara bere a wonhwehwɛ ka da — ɛhia bere sɛ si sika ho nsɛm a wɔgye di a ban tumi gye di.

instruction_en: How do I build a small dam or water harvesting system?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mesi dam ketewa anaa nsuo gye sistɛm?
response_en: Simple water harvesting includes digging half-moon catchments to collect rainwater, constructing check dams as small barriers across drainage channels, and building farm ponds lined with clay or plastic. Contact your local Water Resources Commission for technical support.
response_tw: Nsuo gye anammɔn ketewa ho ne: hu osu nsuo gye mmɔnkyemmonkyem, si check dams te sɛ akwantempon ketewa wɔ nsuo ntwaho nkyɛn, na tu afuo pond a wɔde amane anaa plastik si faako mu. Ka kyerɛ wo Water Resources Commission sɛ wunya nnwuma boa.

instruction_en: My well dries up in the dry season. How can I store more water?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: M'esre yera wɔ ɔpɛ bere mu. Ɛdeɛn na metumi guina nsuo dodo?
response_en: Construct a farm pond or dig a larger deeper well. Install a rainwater harvesting system collecting from rooftops into tanks during rainy season. Use water-efficient irrigation methods. Alternatively identify permanent water sources like rivers and use pumps.
response_tw: Tu afuo pond anaa hu esre kɛse ne tenten. Si osu nsuo gye sistɛm a wɔde fi ato so gye wɔ tank mu wɔ osu bere mu. Fa nsuo ho anammɔn a ɛfa nsuo nkyɛn. Anoa, hwehwɛ nsuo a ɛda hɔ pɛ te sɛ ɔsubɔnten na de pump fa di dwuma.

instruction_en: How do I conserve water on my farm during dry season?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mekora nsuo wɔ m'afuo mu wɔ ɔpɛ bere mu?
response_en: Conserve water by mulching heavily around plants to reduce evaporation, watering early morning or evening only, using drip irrigation which reduces water use by up to 60%, growing drought-tolerant crops, making windbreaks to reduce drying winds, and collecting and storing rainwater during wet season.
response_tw: Kora nsuo wɔ de mulch to nnuaba ho papaapa sɛ nsukyerɛ ba sua, de nsuo to owia da ano anaa anwummere pɛ, fa nsuo tete-tete kyɛ a ɛsua nsuo kɔ 60%, kura nnuaba a ɛtumi nsuo pa ase, si windbreaks sɛ mframa a ɛma asase yera nkɔ, na gye ne guina osu nsuo wɔ osu bere mu.

instruction_en: What basic tools do I need to start a small farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Adeɛ bɛn na mehia sɛ mesi afuo ketewa?
response_en: Essential tools for smallholder farming include a hoe and cutlass for land preparation and weeding, a watering can for irrigation, a knapsack sprayer for pesticide application, a measuring tape, storage bags including hermetic bags for grain, and a simple scale.
response_tw: Adeɛ a ɛhia wɔ afuo ketewa mu ne akɔde ne sekan ma asase siesie ne nhaban tia, nsuo kyɛ ma nsuo to, knapsack sprayer ma dawuro guare, susudua, guina bags a hermetic bags ka ho ma nnuaba, ne scale ketewa ma nnuaba sus.

instruction_en: How do I maintain my knapsack sprayer?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mehwɛ m'knapsack sprayer so?
response_en: After each use, rinse the tank three times with clean water and spray the wash water away from crops. Check and replace worn nozzles regularly. Lubricate the pump piston periodically. Store empty and clean in a cool dry area. Regularly inspect hoses and connections for leaks.
response_tw: Wodi dwuma biara akyi, hohoro nkuku no mprɛ mmiɛnsa wɔ nsuo horo na guare nsuo a wode hohoro no ho afi nnuaba ho. Hwɛ na sesa nozzle a asɛe pɛ. De ngo hyɛ pump no mu bere bi. Guina a ɛhoro wɔ bae a ɛhuru na yera. Hwɛ hose ne nnɔɔmu pɛ.

instruction_en: What does an agricultural extension officer do?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na agricultural extension officer yɛ?
response_en: Agricultural extension officers from MOFA provide free advice and training to farmers. They can visit your farm to diagnose crop problems, recommend solutions, inform you about new varieties and technologies, connect you to government programs, and help you access subsidized inputs.
response_tw: Agricultural extension officers fi MOFA de boa ne nyansapɔ brɛ okuafo akyɛ. Wɔbetumi ba wo afuo so sɛ wuhwɛ nnuaba nsɛm, ka boa kyerɛ, kaa wo tia aba ne technology foforɔ ho, kyerɛ wo ɔman adwuma, na boa wo nya adenneɛ a wɔatwa wɔn boɔ.

instruction_en: How do I access government agricultural subsidies?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na menya ɔman okuafo adwuma boa?
response_en: Register with your local MOFA office and stay in contact with your extension officer. Subsidies like subsidized fertilizer and seeds under PFJ are distributed through MOFA-registered channels. Have your Ghana Card or voter ID ready and register early as subsidies are often limited.
response_tw: Kyerɛw wo din wɔ wo MOFA ofisi a ɛbɛn wo na tena wo extension officer ho. Boa te sɛ ferefere ne aba a wɔatwa boɔ wɔ PFJ ase no pue wɔ MOFA mu. Wura wo Ghana Card anaa ɔman nkyerɛwsɛm na kyerɛw wo din ntɛm efisɛ boa no taa yɛ ketewa.

instruction_en: What is the difference between hybrid seeds and open-pollinated seeds?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Hybrid aba ne open-pollinated aba ntam yɛ dɛn?
response_en: Hybrid seeds are bred from two parent lines to produce vigorous high-yielding crops. However seeds saved from hybrids do not perform well in the next season — you must buy fresh seeds each time. Open-pollinated varieties allow you to save seeds for replanting.
response_tw: Wɔde aba abien ntam bɔ hybrid aba sɛ ɛma nnuaba a wɔwɔ ahoɔden na wɔda sor dodo. Nanso aba a wogye fi hybrid no nnua mu nti adwuma yie bere a ɛto so abien — ɛsɛ sɛ wotɔ aba foforo bere biara. Open-pollinated aba de tumi gye aba sɛ wode to mu bio.
prompt: Question: What is the difference between hybrid seeds and open-pollinated seeds?

instruction_en: How do I save my own seeds for next season?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na megye m'aba sɛ mede to mu wɔ bere a ɛto so?
response_en: For open-pollinated varieties select the best-performing plants and allow their fruits to fully mature on the plant. Harvest seeds, clean and dry them thoroughly in shade. Store in airtight containers with silica gel sachets to absorb moisture. Label with crop name, variety, and date.
response_tw: Open-pollinated aba ho, paw nnua a wɔda sor pa na ma wɔn aba no wie ase yie wɔ nnua so. Gye aba no, hohoro na yaw wɔ owia sum mu yie. Guina wɔ nkuku a apo so ne silica gel sachets a ɛsuo nsukuro. Kyerɛw nnuaba din, aba din ne da so.

instruction_en: How can I farm in a way that helps fight climate change?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na meyɛ okuafo adwuma a ɛboa katawere mu sɛsae so?
response_en: Practice climate-smart farming by planting trees on your farm through agroforestry, using compost and manure instead of chemical fertilizers, practicing minimum tillage to protect soil carbon, using water-efficient irrigation, growing diverse crops, and avoiding burning crop residues.
response_tw: Yɛ climate-smart farming wɔ to nnua wɔ wo afuo so wɔ agroforestry ho, de compost ne aguare fa ahemfie ferefere ananmu, yɛ minimum tillage sɛ ɛkora asase carbon, fa nsuo-efficient irrigation, kura nnuaba ahorow, na bɔ hwɛ hyew nnuaba nsennua.

instruction_en: What crops are most resistant to climate change in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Nnuaba bɛn na ɛtumi wiase tebea sɛsae ase hyɛ yie wɔ Ghana?
response_en: Drought-tolerant crops for Ghana's changing climate include improved NERICA rice, drought-tolerant maize varieties like Obatanpa and Omankwa, cowpea, sorghum, millet, and cassava. Contact CSIR-SARI or MOFA for the latest recommended varieties in your region.
response_tw: Nnuaba a ɛtumi nsuo pa ase hyɛ wɔ Ghana tebea sɛsae mu ne NERICA ɔmɔ a wɔayɛ no foforo, aburow a ɛtumi nsuo pa ase te sɛ Obatanpa ne Omankwa, abɔdweɛ, ɔtɛ, ɔtɛ pa, ne bankye. Ka kyerɛ CSIR-SARI anaa MOFA sɛ wunya aba a wɔaka.

instruction_en: What is agroforestry and how does it benefit my farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na agroforestry yɛ na ɛboa m'afuo sɛn?
response_en: Agroforestry is growing trees alongside crops or livestock on the same land. Benefits include: trees provide shade that reduces water stress, tree roots hold soil and prevent erosion, fallen leaves add organic matter, some trees fix nitrogen improving soil fertility, trees provide fruit or timber as additional income, and trees help store carbon reducing climate change impact. Recommended trees include Moringa, Leucaena, and Acacia.
response_tw: Agroforestry yɛ kura nnua wɔ nnuaba anaa mmoa ho wɔ asase koro so. Mfaaso ho ne: nnua de ewim brɛ ma ɛma nsuo hia nkɔm, nnua ntini kora asase na ɛmma asase nhuru, nhaban a atu de nhwiren foforo ka ho, nnua bi fi nitrogen ma asase ho nhwiren da sor, nnua de aba anaa dua bi ka ho sɛ sika foforɔ, na nnua boa guina carbon na ɛsua katawere mu sɛsae ho ɔkɔm. Nnua a wɔka wɔn ho ne Moringa, Leucaena, ne Acacia.

instruction_en: How do I prepare my farm for unpredictable weather patterns?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mesiesie m'afuo ama ɛwim tebea a ɛntumi nnim?
response_en: To prepare for unpredictable weather: grow more than one crop to spread risk, keep records of past planting dates and yields to understand your local patterns, store enough food and seed reserves, install or improve water harvesting systems, use drought and flood-tolerant varieties, maintain healthy soil with high organic matter which buffers both drought and flooding, and join farmer groups that share weather information.
response_tw: Siesie ama ɛwim tebea a ɛntumi nnim: kura nnuaba dodo sɛ mfomso antete, kora aba to bere ne nnuaba a wogye ho nsɛm sɛ wuhu wo beae tebea, guina aduan ne aba ahwɛso, si anaa siesie nsuo guina systɛm, fa aba a ɛtumi nsuo pa ne nsuo tenten ase hyɛ, kora asase a enni aho yie ne nhwiren foforo dodo a ɛboa wɔ nsuo pa ne nsuo tenten bere, na ba okuafo kuo a wɔkyɛ ɛwim amanneɛ.

instruction_en: What is minimum tillage and why is it good for soil?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na minimum tillage yɛ na ɛdeɛn na ɛyɛ asase pa?
response_en: Minimum tillage (also called conservation tillage) means disturbing the soil as little as possible when preparing for planting — using just enough tillage to create a seedbed. Benefits include: preserves soil structure, reduces erosion, maintains soil moisture, protects soil organisms, reduces fuel and labor costs, and helps store carbon in the soil. Use a jab planter or hand hoe to plant directly into the soil with minimal disturbance.
response_tw: Minimum tillage (a wɔfre no conservation tillage nso) kyerɛ haw asase ketewa ketewa bere a wosiesie aba to mu ansa — fa tillage a ɛdodo pɛ sɛ woyi asase aba to mu ansa. Mfaaso ho ne: kora asase mu siesie, sua erosion, kora nsukuro wɔ asase mu, kora asase mu nkwa, sua fuel ne dwumadie ho sika, na boa guina carbon wɔ asase mu. Fa jab planter anaa nsa akɔde to aba tɔtɔ wɔ asase mu na wonhaw dodo.

instruction_en: How does planting trees on my farm help fight climate change?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na to nnua wɔ m'afuo so boa katawere mu sɛsae so?
response_en: Trees absorb carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and store it in their wood and roots — this is called carbon sequestration. A single large tree can absorb 22 kg of carbon dioxide per year. Trees also reduce local temperatures, increase rainfall through transpiration, protect water catchments, and reduce erosion. Planting trees on your farm is one of the most powerful climate actions a farmer can take.
response_tw: Nnua gye carbon dioxide fi ɛwim mu na guina mu wɔ wɔn dua ne ntini mu — wɔfre eyi carbon sequestration. Dua kɛse baako tumi gye carbon dioxide kilogram 22 wɔ afe biara. Nnua nso sua beae ahuhuro, boa osu ba wɔ transpiration mu, kora nsuo gye beae, na sua erosion. To nnua wɔ wo afuo so yɛ katawere mu sɛsae dwuma titiriw a okuafo tumi yɛ.

instruction_en: What is solar powered irrigation and can small farmers use it?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na solar powered irrigation yɛ na okuafo ketewa tumi di?
response_en: Solar powered irrigation uses solar panels to generate electricity that powers water pumps for irrigating crops. Benefits include: no fuel costs, low maintenance, reliable operation, and environmental friendliness. Small farmers can access it through: government subsidy programs, cooperative ownership of shared solar pumps, microfinance loans for solar pump purchase, and NGO programs. The initial cost is high but long-term savings are significant.
response_tw: Solar powered irrigation fa solar panels di dwuma sɛ ɛyɛ electricity a ɛma nsuo pumps a wɔde to nnuaba so adwuma. Mfaaso ho ne: fuel ho sika nni, hwɛ ase yɛ mmerɛ, adwuma yɛ pɛ, na ɛma ɛwim no da yie. Okuafo ketewa tumi nya ase wɔ: ɔman boa adwuma, cooperative bom fa solar pumps, microfinance ka ma solar pump tɔn, ne NGO adwuma. Sika a wode fi no yɛ den nanso bere tenten ho mfaaso yɛ kɛse.

instruction_en: How can I use my livestock to benefit my crop farm?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mefa m'mmoa di dwuma ma m'afuo nnuaba?
response_en: Integrate livestock by using their manure as organic fertilizer for crops. Chickens can help control insects and weeds. After harvest allow animals to graze on crop residues which also helps clear the field. This integrated farming approach reduces costs and improves productivity.
response_tw: Ka mmoa ho wɔ de wɔn aguare yɛ nhwiren foforo ferefere ma nnuaba. Akrɔma tumi boa kora adwummakers ne nhaban foforo ase. Nnuaba gye akyi, ma mmoa nom nnuaba nsennua a aka no a ɛnso boa hohoro afuo no. Eyi farming anammɔn sua sika na ɛma afuo no nyinaa da sor yie.

instruction_en: My chickens are dying suddenly. What could be wrong?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: M'akrɔma rewu ntɛm. Dɛn na betumi yɛ?
response_en: Sudden chicken deaths could be from Newcastle Disease, Gumboro disease, or poisoning. Vaccinate chickens against common diseases at the right ages. Consult a veterinary officer immediately for diagnosis. Quarantine sick birds to prevent spread to healthy ones.
response_tw: Akrɔma wu a ɛba ntɛm betumi fi Newcastle Yadeɛ, Gumboro yadeɛ, anaa dawuro. De akrɔma kɔ yadeɛ dawuro bere a ɛfata wɔn mfeɛ so. Ka veterinary officer ho ntɛm sɛ ɔhwɛ yadeɛ no. Yi akrɔma a wɔayarɛ no fi wɔn a wɔwɔ aho no mu.

instruction_en: How do I prevent diseases from spreading between my animals?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mema yadeɛ antete wɔ m'mmoa ntam?
response_en: Vaccinate all animals on schedule, quarantine new animals for 2 weeks before mixing with your herd, isolate sick animals immediately, maintain clean housing with good ventilation, dispose of dead animals properly, and consult a veterinary officer regularly. Good hygiene is the most important disease prevention tool.
response_tw: De mmoa nyinaa kɔ yadeɛ dawuro bere a ɛsɛ, yi mmoa foforo na wɔntena nnawotwe 2 ansa na woka wɔn ne mmoa a wɔwɔ hɔ bom, yi mmoa a wɔayarɛ ntɛm ara, kora wɔn fie a ɛhoro ne mframa pue mu yie, yi mmoa a awu yie, na ka veterinary officer ho pɛ.

instruction_en: What farming apps are useful for farmers in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Farming apps bɛn na ɛboa okuafo wɔ Ghana?
response_en: Useful apps for Ghanaian farmers include: Esoko for market prices, Farmerline for agricultural advice and services, Hello Tractor for renting tractors, AgroFresh for produce quality management, Google Translate for translating agricultural content, YouTube for farming tutorials, Ghana Met Agency app for weather forecasts, and WhatsApp for connecting with other farmers and extension officers. Most are free or low-cost.
response_tw: Apps a ɛboa okuafo Ghana mu ne: Esoko ma aguabasa boɔ, Farmerline ma okuafo adwuma boa ne nnwuma, Hello Tractor ma tractor asɛ, AgroFresh ma nnuaba ho pa hwɛ, Google Translate ma okuafo nsɛm dan, YouTube ma okuafo adwuma nhyehyɛe, Ghana Met Agency app ma ɛwim amanneɛ, ne WhatsApp ma kasa ne okuafo ne extension officers foforɔ. Wɔn bebree yɛ daa anaa boɔ yɛ ketewa.

instruction_en: How can I use YouTube to learn farming techniques?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mefa YouTube di dwuma sɛ sua okuafo adwuma anammɔn?
response_en: YouTube has thousands of free farming tutorials. Search for: specific crop names plus 'farming Ghana', pest or disease names plus 'how to control', fertilizer application techniques, irrigation setup guides, and post-harvest handling. Subscribe to channels like IITA, CTA Agriculture, and local Ghanaian farming channels. Download videos on WiFi to watch offline when you are at the farm without internet access.
response_tw: YouTube wɔ okuafo adwuma nhyehyɛe ahorow a ɛyɛ daa. Hwehwɛ: nnuaba din ne 'farming Ghana', adwumakers anaa yadeɛ din ne 'how to control', ferefere to anammɔn, nsuo to nhyehyɛe, ne gye akyi hwɛ ase anammɔn. Kyerɛw wo din wɔ channels te sɛ IITA, CTA Agriculture, ne Ghana okuafo channels. Sii videos wɔ WiFi so sɛ wuhwɛ wɔn bere a wowɔ afuo so na internet nni hɔ.

instruction_en: What is a soil testing kit and how do I use it?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na asase hwɛ kit yɛ na ɛdeɛn na mefa di dwuma?
response_en: A soil testing kit is a set of chemicals and test strips or color charts that let you test your soil's pH, nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium levels at home. To use: collect soil from several spots in your field and mix together, take a small sample, follow the kit instructions to mix with testing chemicals, compare the color change to the chart provided, and use results to decide what fertilizer to apply. Kits are available at agricultural supply stores.
response_tw: Asase hwɛ kit yɛ chemicals ne test strips anaa color charts a ɛma wo tumi hwɛ wo asase pH, nitrogen, phosphorus, ne potassium wɔ fie. Di dwuma eyi: gye asase fi beae ahodoɔ wɔ afuo mu na ka bom, fa sample ketewa, bu kit nhyɛso so sɛ de chemicals ka ho, twe rere sɛsae na ka ho ne chart a wɔde brɛ, na fa adwuma ho sɛ paw ferefere a wohia. Kits wɔ hɔ wɔ agricultural supply adeɛtɔ hɔ.

Kits wɔ hɔ wɔ agricultural supply adeɛtɔ hɔ.

instruction_en: What is the role of women in agriculture in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na mmaa gyina wɔ okuafo adwuma mu wɔ Ghana?
response_en: Women play a critical role in Ghanaian agriculture — they make up about 52% of the agricultural workforce. Women are primarily responsible for food crop production, post-harvest processing, and marketing. They often face challenges accessing land, credit, and inputs. Programs that specifically support women farmers include Women in Agricultural Development (WIAD) under MOFA, and many NGOs focusing on women's empowerment in agriculture.
response_tw: Mmaa gyina titiriw pa ara wɔ Ghana okuafo adwuma mu — wɔyɛ bɛn 52% wɔ okuafo adwumafo mu. Mmaa hia aduan nnuaba kura, gye akyi yɛ adwuma, ne tɔn. Wɔtaa hia asase, ka, ne adenneɛ ho nsɛm. Adwuma a ɛhwɛ mmaa okuafo ho pɛ ne Women in Agricultural Development (WIAD) wɔ MOFA ase, ne NGOs bebree a wɔhwɛ mmaa ho wɔ okuafo adwuma mu.

instruction_en: How do I use a smartphone camera to help diagnose crop problems?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mefa smartphone camera di dwuma sɛ hunu m'nnuaba nsɛm?
response_en: Take clear close-up photos of affected plant parts — leaves, stems, fruits, or roots. Include a coin for scale. Send photos to: your extension officer via WhatsApp, post in farmer WhatsApp or Facebook groups for advice, use apps like Plantix or PlantVillage that use AI to diagnose diseases from photos, or email to your nearest CSIR research station. Clear photos from multiple angles help get the most accurate diagnosis.
response_tw: Fa foto fitafita wɔ nnuaba a wɔayarɛ no ho — nhaban, tibo, aba, anaa ntini. Ka sika de hwɛ boɔ. De foto kɔ: wo extension officer ho wɔ WhatsApp, de si wɔ okuafo WhatsApp anaa Facebook kuo mu sɛ wunya boa, fa apps te sɛ Plantix anaa PlantVillage a wɔde AI di dwuma sɛ hunu yadeɛ fi foto mu, anaa email kɔ CSIR research station a ɛbɛn wo. Foto fitafita fi afã ahodoɔ boa sɛ wuhu yadeɛ no pa ara.

instruction_en: How do I start a small poultry farm at home?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mesi akrɔma afuo ketewa wɔ fie?
response_en: To start a small home poultry farm: decide on type (layers for eggs or broilers for meat), build or buy a simple wooden or wire coop with shade and good ventilation, start with 10-20 birds, provide clean water and commercial feed, vaccinate birds at the recommended ages, keep the coop clean, and record feeds, deaths, and egg production. Start small and scale as you learn.
response_tw: Sɛ wohwehwɛ si akrɔma afuo ketewa wɔ fie: paw ahodoɔ (layers ma kosua anaa broilers ma nam), si anaa tɔ dua anaa wire fie a ewim brɛ ne mframa pue mu yie, fi akrɔma 10-20, de nsuo horo ne aduan a wɔtɔ ma wɔn, de akrɔma kɔ yadeɛ dawuro bere a ɛsɛ, kora fie no horo, na kora aduan, wuu, ne kosua ho nsɛm. Fi ketewa na fa dodo so bere a wosua.

instruction_en: How do I improve the quality of milk from my cows?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na metumi ma m'boo nufuo pa ara?
response_en: Improve cow milk quality and quantity by: feeding balanced rations including good pasture, silage, hay, and concentrate feed; providing clean water always; milking at the same time every day; maintaining good hygiene during milking; treating and preventing mastitis (udder inflammation) promptly; providing shade and reducing stress; and selecting productive dairy breeds or crossing local cattle with improved breeds.
response_tw: Siesie boo nufuo ho pa ne kɛse wɔ: ma aduan a ɛpɛ wɔn ho yie a mu wɔ atɛntɛnni pa, silage, hay, ne concentrate aduan; de nsuo horo ma wɔn daa; nom wɔn nufuo bere koro da biara; kora ho tumi bere a wonuom nufuo; yɛ dawuro ma na kora mastitis (nufuo mu yadeɛ) ntɛm; ma ewim brɛ wɔn na mma wɔn nhaw; na paw boo a wɔn nufuo yɛ pa anaa ka beae boo ne boo foforɔ a ɛda sor bom.

instruction_en: What is the best local feed I can give my livestock to reduce costs?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Aduan bɛn a ɛwɔ fie na ɛyɛ pa wɔ m'mmoa ho sɛ mpampa sika?
response_en: Reduce feed costs using local ingredients: for poultry, use maize germ, rice bran, palm kernel cake, fishmeal, and dried moringa leaves as protein; for pigs, use cassava peels, plantain peels, vegetable waste, and kitchen leftovers (no meat or salt); for cattle, plant improved pasture grasses like Brachiaria and Stylosanthes, use crop residues like maize stover and rice straw, and supplement with salt blocks.
response_tw: Sua aduan sika wɔ fa beae adenneɛ: akrɔma ho, fa aburow germ, ɔmɔ nkutupan, abe nkuto cake, nsuomnam dust, ne moringa nhaban a ayaw sɛ aduan protein; prako ho, fa bankye nkutupan, borɔdɔ nkutupan, nnuan asennua, ne fie aduan a aka (mfa nam anaa ɛwa); boo ho, to Brachiaria ne Stylosanthes atɛntɛnni a wɔayɛ wɔn foforo, fa nnuaba nsennua te sɛ aburow nsennua ne ɔmɔ tow, na ka salt blocks ho.

instruction_en: How do I handle sick animals safely?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mehwɛ mmoa a wɔayarɛ ho yie?
response_en: When handling sick animals: wear gloves and wash hands thoroughly after contact, isolate sick animals from healthy ones immediately, do not move sick animals to other farms, contact a veterinary officer for diagnosis before administering treatment, follow withdrawal periods for medicines before slaughter or selling milk, keep records of all treatments given, and dispose of dead animals by deep burial away from water sources.
response_tw: Bere a wuhwɛ mmoa a wɔayarɛ ho: fa nsatea nhoma na hohoro wo nsa yie akyi a woka wɔn, yi mmoa a wɔayarɛ fi wɔn a wɔwɔ aho no mu ntɛm, mfa mmoa a wɔayarɛ kɔ afuo foforɔ, ka veterinary officer ho sɛ ɔhunu yadeɛ no ansa na wode dawuro to, bu dawuro withdrawal bere ansa na wokum anaa tɔn nufuo, kora nsɛm a yɛ dawuro nyinaa ho, na de mmoa a awu sisie wɔ asase mu tenten fi nsuo ho.

instruction_en: What are the signs that my goat is pregnant and how do I care for it?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ahohyenam bɛn na ɛkyerɛ sɛ m'abirekyi wɔ nyinsɛn na ɛdeɛn na meyɛ?
response_en: Signs of goat pregnancy include: no return to heat after mating, enlarged abdomen especially from month 3, enlarged udder especially in the last month, behavioral changes like becoming calmer, and weight gain. Care during pregnancy: provide extra feed especially in the last 6 weeks, ensure access to clean water always, avoid rough handling, vaccinate against clostridial diseases, deworm at least 3 weeks before kidding, and prepare a clean kidding area.
response_tw: Abirekyi nyinsɛn ahohyenam ho ne: onsan nba bio akyi a watwa, yafunu kɛse titiriw fi bosome 3, nufuo kɛse titiriw wɔ bosome ɔkyena, ɔtenabea sɛsae te sɛ yɛ abotare, na boɔ sor. Hwɛ bere nyinsɛn mu: ma aduan dodo titiriw wɔ nnawotwe 6 a atwam ansa na omba, kora nsuo horo wɔ hɔ daa, mhaw no, de kɔ clostridial yadeɛ dawuro, hwɛ adwummakers ase wiemu 3 ansa na oba mba, na siesie beae horo sɛ oba mba.

instruction_en: What food safety practices should farmers follow?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Aduan ho tumi anammɔn bɛn na okuafo sɛ bu wɔn so?
response_en: Key food safety practices for farmers include: washing hands before handling produce, using clean water for irrigation especially on leafy vegetables, applying pesticides according to label instructions and respecting pre-harvest intervals, not using human waste as fertilizer, keeping animals away from vegetable gardens, using clean equipment and containers, and training farm workers on hygiene practices.
response_tw: Aduan ho tumi anammɔn a ɛhia ma okuafo ne: hohoro wo nsa ansa na wohwɛ nnuaba ho, fa nsuo horo sɛ to nnuaba so titiriw nhaban nnuan so, de adwummakers dawuro to wɔ krataa nhyɛso so na bu pre-harvest bere so, mfa onipa aguare yɛ ferefere, mma mmoa nnuaba afuo mu, fa egyapade ne nkuku horo, na kyerɛ afuo adwumafo aduan ho tumi anammɔn.

instruction_en: How long should I wait after spraying pesticide before harvesting?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Bere ahe na ɛsɛ me twɛn akyi a mede adwummakers dawuro guare ansa na megye nnuaba?
response_en: The waiting time between pesticide application and harvest is called the Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) and varies by chemical. Always check the pesticide label for the specific PHI. Common examples: Emamectin benzoate (3-7 days), Cypermethrin (7-14 days), Mancozeb fungicide (7 days), Glyphosate herbicide (14 days). If the label is lost, wait at least 14 days as a safe default. Never harvest before the PHI has passed.
response_tw: Bere a ɛda adwummakers dawuro to ne nnuaba gye ntam sɛ Pre-Harvest Interval (PHI) na ɛsesa wɔ chemical biara so. Hwɛ adwummakers dawuro krataa so bere a ɛhia pɛ. Nhwɛso a wɔtaa hu: Emamectin benzoate (nnafoa 3-7), Cypermethrin (nnafoa 7-14), Mancozeb fungicide (nnafoa 7), Glyphosate herbicide (nnafoa 14). Sɛ krataa no ahu a, twɛn nnafoa 14 pɛ sɛ beae a ɛho tumi. Mye nnuaba ansa na PHI no awi da.

instruction_en: How do I wash vegetables safely before eating or selling?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mehohoro nnuan yie ansa na midi anaa tɔn?
response_en: Wash vegetables safely by: using clean potable water, soaking for 1-2 minutes in a solution of 1 tablespoon of vinegar per litre of water or approved food-grade chlorine solution, then rinsing thoroughly with clean water. For leafy vegetables, separate leaves and wash each leaf individually. Dry before packaging. Never wash with river, pond, or canal water as these may contain pathogens.
response_tw: Hohoro nnuan yie wɔ: fa nsuo horo a wɔtumi nom, da wɔ vinegar tablespoon baako wɔ nsuo litre biara mu anaa food-grade chlorine a wɔagyei mu sɛntɔ 1-2, afei hohoro yie wɔ nsuo horo. Nhaban nnuan ho, twa nhaban mu na hohoro nhaban biara biara ho. Yaw ansa na wopaka. Mhohoro wɔ ɔsubɔnten, asranom, anaa kɔtɔ nsuo mu da efisɛ wɔn mu tumi wɔ yadeɛ.

instruction_en: What is the danger of using raw human or animal waste as fertilizer?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na ɔkɔm wɔ ho wɔ fa onipa anaa mmoa aguare foforo yɛ ferefere?
response_en: Using raw (untreated) waste carries serious risks: human and animal waste contains pathogens like E.coli, Salmonella, and parasites that can contaminate crops and cause disease in people who eat them. The WHO recommends composting waste for at least 6-12 months or treating with ash or lime before applying to crops. Only apply to soil, never to the edible parts of plants. Always wash hands and produce thoroughly.
response_tw: Fa aguare foforo (a wonsi amu) di dwuma wɔ ɔkɔm kɛse: onipa ne mmoa aguare wɔ yadeɛ mu te sɛ E.coli, Salmonella, ne adwumakers a ɛtumi haw nnuaba na yɛ nnipa a wɔdi wɔn yadee. WHO ka sɛ compost aguare wiemu 6-12 anaa siesie wɔ nkamfo anaa lime ho ansa na wode to nnuaba so. De to asase pɛ, mde to nnuaba a wɔdi ho da. Hohoro wo nsa ne nnuaba yie daa.

instruction_en: How do I report a pesticide poisoning emergency?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mede adwummakers dawuro ɔkɔm hwe krataa kɔ?
response_en: If pesticide poisoning occurs: call the Ghana Poisons Information Centre or the nearest hospital immediately, remove the victim from the exposure area, if ingested do not induce vomiting unless directed by medical professionals, if on skin remove contaminated clothing and wash with soap and water, if in eyes flush with clean water for 15-20 minutes, bring the pesticide container or label to the hospital. Prevention: always wear protective equipment when using pesticides.
response_tw: Sɛ adwummakers dawuro ɔkɔm ba a: frɛ Ghana Poisons Information Centre anaa sikabea a ɛbɛn wo ntɛm, yi obi a ɔkɔm aba no fi beae no, sɛ ɔnom a mma onyi no mua ansa na onyini ɔkɔm ne mu, sɛ firi wo ho skin so a yi ntama a dawuro ato so na hohoro wɔ sabɔ ne nsuo mu, sɛ wɔ ani mu a fa nsuo horo mu sɛntɔ 15-20, de adwummakers dawuro nkuku anaa krataa kɔ sikabea. Kora ase: taa hye ntoma a ɛkora wo ho bere a wode adwummakers dawuro di dwuma.

completion: If pesticide poisoning occurs: call the Ghana Poisons Information Centre or the nearest hospital immediately, remove the victim from the exposure area, if ingested do not induce vomiting unless directed by medical professionals, if on skin remove contaminated clothing and wash with soap and water, if in eyes flush with clean water for 15-20 minutes, bring the pesticide container or label to the hospital. Prevention: always wear protective equipment when using pesticides.
prompt_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mede adwummakers dawuro ɔkɔm hwe krataa kɔ?
completion_tw: Sɛ adwummakers dawuro ɔkɔm ba a: frɛ Ghana Poisons Information Centre anaa sikabea a ɛbɛn wo ntɛm, yi obi a ɔkɔm aba no fi beae no, sɛ ɔnom a mma onyi no mua ansa na onyini ɔkɔm ne mu, sɛ firi wo ho skin so a yi ntama a dawuro ato so na hohoro wɔ sabɔ ne nsuo mu, sɛ wɔ ani mu a fa nsuo horo mu sɛntɔ 15-20, de adwummakers dawuro nkuku anaa krataa kɔ sikabea. Kora ase: taa hye ntoma a ɛkora wo ho bere a wode adwummakers dawuro di dwuma.

instruction_en: Where can I buy quality certified seeds in Ghana?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛhe na metumi tɔ aba a wɔakyerɛw wɔn wɔ Ghana?
response_en: Buy certified seeds from: MOFA-approved agro-input dealers, agricultural supply stores in district capitals, certified seed companies like SeedCo, Pioneer, and Yara, cooperative input stores, and CSIR research stations which sometimes sell breeder or foundation seeds. Always look for the official seed certification label on the bag. Avoid buying seeds from roadsides or markets where quality cannot be guaranteed.
response_tw: Tɔ aba a wɔakyerɛw wɔn fi: agro-input adeɛtɔfo a MOFA agyei, agricultural supply adeɛtɔ wɔ district titirimpon mu, aba kuo a wɔakyerɛw wɔn te sɛ SeedCo, Pioneer, ne Yara, cooperative input adeɛtɔ, ne CSIR research stations a wɔtaa tɔn breeder anaa foundation aba. Hwɛ daa aba kyerɛwsɛm krataa a ɛwɔ bag so no. Bɔ hwɛ tɔ aba fi kwan ho anaa aguabasa a wɔntumi nkyerɛ aho hɔ.

instruction_en: What is seed priming and how does it help germination?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Dɛn na seed priming yɛ na ɛboa aba pue sɛn?
response_en: Seed priming is soaking seeds in water or a special solution for a specific time before planting to improve germination rate, speed, and uniformity. Methods include: hydropriming (soaking in plain water for 6-12 hours then drying before planting), and salt priming (soaking in dilute salt solution). Primed seeds germinate faster and more uniformly, which helps crops establish better especially in marginal soils or unpredictable rainfall.
response_tw: Seed priming kyerɛ da aba wɔ nsuo anaa aduru a wɔakyerɛ ho bere mu ansa na woto mu sɛ ɛsiesie aba pue rate, ntɛm, ne pɛ. Anammɔn ho ne: hydropriming (da wɔ nsuo foforo mu dapɛn 6-12 na yaw ansa na woto), ne salt priming (da wɔ ɛwa a wɔasusu mu). Aba a wɔaprime no pue ntɛm ne pɛ, eyi boa nnuaba si yie titiriw wɔ asase a enni pa anaa osu a ɛntumi nnim bere mu.

instruction_en: How do I know if seeds are still viable and worth planting?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na menim sɛ aba no da sor na ɛfata sɛ mede to mu?
response_en: Test seed viability by: placing 10 seeds on a damp paper towel, fold and keep in a warm place, check after 7-10 days. Count how many germinated — if 8-10 germinated (80-100%) seeds are excellent; 6-7 (60-70%) are acceptable; below 5 (50%) plant more seeds per hole. Old or poorly stored seeds lose viability — check the production date on the bag and do not use seeds more than 2 years old.
response_tw: Hwɛ aba ho pa wɔ: de aba 10 si krataa nte so, fa bo mu na guina wɔ bae a ɛhuru, hwɛ nnafoa 7-10 akyi. Kan a pue — sɛ 8-10 apue (80-100%) a aba no yɛ pa pa ara; 6-7 (60-70%) akyɛ; ɛsua 5 (50%) a to aba dodo wɔ tumtum biara mu. Aba kedee anaa a wonguina wɔn yie sua wɔn tumi — hwɛ yɛ da a ɛwɔ bag so na mfa aba a wɔadin mfe abien kenkan.

instruction_en: How can young people make farming profitable?
instruction_tw: Asemmisa: Ɛdeɛn na mmofra tumi ma okuafo adwuma brɛ sika?
response_en: Young people can make farming profitable by: treating farming as a business not a subsistence activity, specializing in high-value crops or livestock products, using technology and innovation, accessing youth-specific agricultural finance programs, connecting to urban markets through digital platforms, forming youth cooperatives for scale and bargaining power, adding value through processing, and combining farming with agribusiness services like input supply or transportation.
response_tw: Mmofra tumi ma okuafo adwuma brɛ sika wɔ: hwɛ okuafo adwuma sɛ adwuma na mfa sɛ fie aduan adwuma, paw nnuaba anaa mmoa nnuaba ho adwuma a wɔtɔ boɔ kɛse, fa technology ne nnorimde di dwuma, nya mmofra ho okuafo adwuma ka adwuma, ka kɔ akura aguabasa wɔ digital platforms ho, si mmofra cooperative sɛ fa ɔman ne tumi kɛse, de boɔ ka ho wɔ yɛ adwuma mu, na ka okuafo adwuma ho nnwuma te sɛ adenneɛ tɔn anaa kwan so nnwuma.
